target.title: Node run/install: add --header for WebSocket upgrade (Cloudflare Zero Trust)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw node run` and `node install` had no way to pass custom HTTP headers on the WebSocket upgrade request, causing silent 302 redirects and connection failures when the node gateway sits behind Cloudflare Zero Trust or similar auth proxies
- **Why it matters:** Teams deploying OpenClaw in Zero Trust network environments (common in enterprise) were completely blocked — the WS handshake would redirect instead of upgrading, with no workaround
- **What changed:** Added a repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` CLI flag to both `node run` and `node install`; headers are persisted to config and forwarded to the gateway WebSocket client; also supports `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, and `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env vars for the installed daemon service
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to gateway routing logic, auth token handling, skill execution, or any behavior for users not passing `--header`

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42792
- Related: none

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `openclaw node run` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags
- `openclaw node install` now accepts repeatable `--header "Name: Value"` flags; headers are written to config and used by the installed daemon on startup
- New env vars supported: `OPENCLAW_NODE_HEADERS` (JSON), `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_ID`, `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET`
- No behavior change for users who do not pass `--header`

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `Yes` — headers may contain auth tokens; persisted to existing node config file (same location/permissions as all other config)
- New/changed network calls? `Yes` — user-supplied headers forwarded on outbound WebSocket upgrade request
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- **Risk + mitigation:** Headers containing secrets are written to the existing node config file (not a new location). No headers are logged or surfaced in output. The `CF_ACCESS_CLIENT_SECRET` env var name makes sensitivity clear to users.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.x (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node.js v22, pnpm monorepo
- Model/provider: N/A (gateway connectivity issue, not model-specific)
- Integration/channel: Cloudflare Zero Trust (simulated via mock HTTP proxy)
- Relevant config: none required

### Steps

**1. Start a mock proxy that returns 302 if the CF header is absent:**
```js
const http = require('http');
const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  if (!req.headers['cf-access-client-id']) {
    res.writeHead(302, { Location: 'https://example.cloudflareaccess.com' });
    res.end();
    return;
  }
  res.writeHead(200);
  res.end();
});
server.listen(9999, () => console.log('Mock proxy listening on 9999'));
```

**2. Without fix — run in second terminal:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999
```

**3. With fix — run with headers:**
```bash
pnpm openclaw node run --host localhost --port 9999 \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Id: test123" \
  --header "CF-Access-Client-Secret: secret456"
```

### Expected

- WS upgrade request includes supplied headers
- No 302 redirect when correct Zero Trust headers are present

### Actual (before fix)

- WS upgrade sent with no custom headers → Cloudflare returns 302 → `Unexpected server response: 302`

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

**Before fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 302
```

**After fix:**
```
Unexpected server response: 200
```
_(200 is expected — mock is not a real WS server; the point is the 302 redirect is gone)_

New unit tests added:
- `src/gateway/client.test.ts` — headers passed to WS constructor
- `src/daemon/program-args.test.ts` — `--header` args emitted correctly
- `src/cli/node-cli/header-args.test.ts` — parse logic including edge cases

All 895 test files, 7301 tests passing. `pnpm check` clean.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:**
  - `node run` with single `--header` → header present in WS upgrade
  - `node run` with multiple `--header` flags → all headers forwarded
  - `node run` with no `--header` → behavior identical to pre-fix
  - Mock proxy 302→200 transition confirmed end-to-end
  - `pnpm check` and `pnpm test` both clean on branch
- **Edge cases checked:**
  - Malformed header string (missing `:`) → `parseHeaderArgs` throws with clear error
  - Header value containing `:` (e.g. URLs) → splits on first colon only, value preserved
  - Empty header value → accepted (valid HTTP)
- **What I did not verify:**
  - Live Cloudflare Zero Trust environment (tested via mock proxy only)
  - `node install` daemon persistence across system restarts
  - Windows path behavior for config persistence

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — `--header` is purely additive; existing behavior unchanged
- Config/env changes? `Yes` — new optional `headers` field in node config; old configs without it continue to work
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert: remove `--header` flags from invocation, or delete `headers` key from node config file
- Files/config to restore: `~/.openclaw/node-config.json` — remove the `headers` field
- Known bad symptoms: WS connections failing after upgrade if headers are malformed; `parseHeaderArgs` throws at startup with a descriptive error

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** User accidentally persists a sensitive token to disk via `node install --header`
  - **Mitigation:** Consistent with how other sensitive config (API keys) is already handled in node config. No new attack surface; file permissions unchanged.
- **Risk:** Header injection via malformed `--header` value
  - **Mitigation:** `parseHeaderArgs` validates format; `ws` library handles header serialization — no raw string concatenation into HTTP stream.

target.title: fix(skills): list omitted skill names when prompt is truncated
target.body:
## Problem

When many skills are installed (e.g. 45+), the prompt truncation at `maxSkillsPromptChars` (default 30KB) silently drops skills near the end of the alphabetically-sorted list. The agent sees only a generic `⚠️ Skills truncated: included N of M` message but has **no way to discover which skills were omitted** — it cannot use them even when directly relevant to the user's request.

Real-world example: a user sends a `mp.weixin.qq.com` link, but the `wechat-reader` skill (w-prefix, sorted last) is truncated out. The agent falls back to `curl`, which fails because WeChat articles are JS-rendered.

## Solution

When skills are truncated, the note now includes the **names** of omitted skills (capped at 1KB to stay compact) and suggests `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` so the agent can still discover and read them on demand.

**Before:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

**After:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit. Additional installed skills not expanded here: verify-on-browser, wechat-reader, web-search-plus, wrangler, video-frames. If a listed skill name becomes relevant, inspect it with `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` before deciding whether to read its SKILL.md.
```

## Changes

- `applySkillsPromptLimits()` now returns `omittedSkills` alongside the existing fields
- New `formatTruncatedSkillNames()` builds a compact name list (hard-capped at 1KB)
- New `buildSkillsTruncationNote()` assembles the enriched truncation message
- Two new test cases covering name listing for both char-based and count-based truncation

## Tests

All 34 existing + new tests pass (`npx vitest run src/agents/skills`).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Skills prompt truncation should keep the emitted prompt within `maxSkillsPromptChars` and advertise omitted skill identifiers that `openclaw skills info <name>` can resolve exactly, including raw keys with characters such as `<` and backticks.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Darwin arm64, Node v22.22.2, pnpm 10.33.2, branch `fix/skills-truncation-visibility`, current HEAD `db926508c4`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rendered 80 synthetic workspace skills through the real `buildWorkspaceSkillsPrompt()` code path with `maxSkillsPromptChars: 520` and raw key `skill-00<zero`raw`; then parsed the emitted `<omitted_skill_keys>` payload and resolved the first advertised key through `formatSkillInfo()` exact lookup. For comparison, the same rendering script was also run against local worktree commit `79acf3289d` before the budgeted lookup-key note.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output copied from the local command run.

```text
Before (79acf3289d):
{
  "commit": "before",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 989,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": false,
  "advertisedCount": 0,
  "advertisedFirst": null,
  "hasRawKey": false
}
--- prompt excerpt ---
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 0 of 80 (compact format, descriptions omitted). Omitted: skill-00zeroraw, skill-01, skill-02, skill-03, skill-04, skill-05, skill-06, skill-07, skill-08, skill-09, skill-10, skill-11, skill-12, skill-13, skill-14, skill-15, skill-16, skill-17, skill-18, skill-19, skill-20, skill-21, skill-22, skill-23, skill-24, skill-25, skill-26, skill-27, skill-28, skill-29, skill-30, skill-31, skill-32, skill-33, skill-34, skill-35, skill-36, skill-37, skill-38, skill-39, skill-40, skill-41, skill-42, skill-43, skill-44, skill-45, skill-46, skill-47, skill-48, skill-49, skill-50, skill-51, skill-52, skill-53, skill-54, skill-55, skill-56, skill-57, skill-58, skill-59, skill-60, skill-61, skill-62, skill-63, skill-64, skill-65, skill-66, skill-67, skill-68, skill-69, skill-70, skill-71, skill-72, skill-73, skill-74, skill-75, skill-76, skill-77, skill-78, skill-79. Use `openclaw skills info <name>` to load them on demand. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.

After (db926508c4):
{
  "commit": "after",

Omitted skill lookup keys for `openclaw skills info <name>` (JSON data; use exact string values): <omitted_skill_keys>["skill-00\u003czero`raw","skill-01","skill-02","skill-03","skill-04","skill-05","skill-06","skill-07","skill-08","skill-09","skill-10","skill-11","skill-12","skill-13","skill-14","skill-15","skill-16","skill-17","skill-18","skill-19","skill-20"]</omitted_skill_keys> (+59 more) Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.

What was not tested: Live model submission was not needed for this prompt-rendering fix; the local proof covers the real prompt builder and CLI exact lookup formatter path.

target.title: fix(control-ui): wire ui.seamColor from bootstrap config to CSS variables
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #56068. The `ui.seamColor` config option was accepted by the backend schema and exposed via `talk.config`, but the Control UI frontend ignored it because:

1. The HTTP bootstrap payload (`/__openclaw/control-ui-config.json`) never included `seamColor`
2. The frontend loader never read or applied it to CSS custom properties

This PR wires `seamColor` end-to-end: gateway bootstrap contract → HTTP response → frontend state → inline `:root` CSS variable overrides.

## Changes

- `src/gateway/control-ui-contract.ts`: add `seamColor?: string` to `ControlUiBootstrapConfig`
- `src/gateway/control-ui.ts`: forward `config?.ui?.seamColor` in the bootstrap payload
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/control-ui-bootstrap.ts`:
  - add `seamColor` to `ControlUiBootstrapState`
  - add `applySeamColor()` helper that sets `--accent`, `--accent-hover`, `--accent-muted`, `--accent-subtle`, `--accent-glow`, `--ring`, `--primary`, `--focus` via `document.documentElement.style`
  - removes those properties when `seamColor` is absent so `base.css` hardcoded defaults win
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/control-ui-bootstrap.test.ts`: add tests for CSS variable application, cleanup, hashless hex normalization, and complete removal coverage

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Issue #56068 — the configured `ui.seamColor` is now applied as the Control UI accent color; removing it falls back to the default red.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64, Node v22.19.0, Chrome 148.0.7778.215 headless, local OpenClaw source checkout.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Add `"ui": { "seamColor": "#00aaff" }` to `~/.openclaw-dev/openclaw.json`
  2. Run `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs --dev gateway`
  3. Open `http://localhost:19001/` in Chrome headless and dump the DOM root style
  4. Verify the bootstrap endpoint returns `seamColor` and the root element carries the derived CSS variables
  5. Remove `seamColor` from config, restart gateway, refresh page
  6. Verify the CSS variables are removed and the default red accent returns
- **Evidence after fix**:
  - Bootstrap endpoint returns `seamColor`:
    ```text
    $ curl -s http://localhost:19001/__openclaw/control-ui-config.json | python3 -m json.tool
    {
        "basePath": "",
        "assistantName": "C3-PO",
        "assistantAvatar": "🤖",
        "assistantAvatarSource": "avatars/c3po.png",
        "assistantAvatarStatus": "none",
        "assistantAvatarReason": "missing",
        "assistantAgentId": "dev",
        "serverVersion": "2026.6.2",
        "localMediaPreviewRoots": [
            "/tmp/openclaw",
            "/home/0668000666/.openclaw-dev/media",
            "/home/0668000666/.openclaw-dev/canvas",
            "/home/0668000666/.openclaw-dev/workspace",
            "/home/0668000666/.openclaw-dev/sandboxes",
            "/home/0668000666/.openclaw/workspace-dev"
        ],
        "embedSandbox": "scripts",
        "allowExternalEmbedUrls": false,
        "seamColor": "#00aaff"
    }
    ```
  - The configured blue accent is applied to the `:root` style (Chrome headless DOM dump):
    ```text
    $ google-chrome --headless --disable-gpu --no-sandbox --window-size=1280,720 \
        --virtual-time-budget=5000 --dump-dom http://localhost:19001/ \
        | grep -o 'style="[^"]*"' | head -n 1
    style="color-scheme: light; --radius-sm: 6px; --radius-md: 10px; --radius-lg: 14px; --radius-xl: 20px; --radius-full: 9999px; --radius: 10px; --control-ui-text-scale: 1.00; --accent: #00aaff; --accent-hover: color-mix(in srgb, #00aaff, white 15%); --accent-muted: #00aaff; --accent-subtle: rgba(0, 170, 255, 0.1); --accent-glow: rgba(0, 170, 255, 0.2); --ring: #00aaff; --primary: #00aaff; --focus: #00aaff;"
    ```
  - After removing `seamColor` and restarting the gateway, the derived variables are absent from `:root` and the default red accent returns (fallback screenshot):

    ![Control UI with default red seamColor](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/eae8f153-81f8-4ce1-b6ef-e2d29bae29cd)
- **Observed result after fix**: The bootstrap endpoint returns the configured `seamColor`, the `:root` element receives the derived blue CSS variables, and the Control UI renders blue accents. After removing `seamColor` and restarting the gateway, the variables are removed and the default red accent returns.
- **What was not tested**: Dark/light theme switching behavior; WebSocket `talk.config` path (already worked); live model chat (no API key in the test environment).

## Verification

- Unit tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/controllers/control-ui-bootstrap.test.ts` → 13 passed
- Build: `pnpm build` → passed
- Lint: `npx oxlint --import-plugin --config .oxlintrc.json src/gateway/control-ui-contract.ts src/gateway/control-ui.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/control-ui-bootstrap.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/control-ui-bootstrap.test.ts` → clean

target.title: Polish Workboard operations view
target.body:
## Summary
- polish the Workboard operations UI across card layout, filters, health metrics, details, responsive behavior, dropdowns, and tooltips
- add guarded auto-refresh, serialized writes, linked-task reconciliation, bounded task discovery, and safer start/stop behavior
- preserve accessible names, selected custom-select values, tooltip viewport behavior, and native-title fallback semantics
- expand focused controller/view/tooltip/lifecycle coverage and deterministic Workboard browser E2E

## Maintenance repairs
- rebased onto current Workboard base behavior while preserving main's hide-empty-column and accessibility changes
- deferred failed lifecycle refresh retries, preserved matching task links on enrichment failure, and committed poll results atomically so in-flight polls cannot disrupt drag-and-drop
- hardened canonical/replacement task matching, terminal discovery cursor cycling, unresolved-start tracking, and task/session stop fallback behavior
- kept session-only lifecycle reconciliation available when best-effort task discovery fails, while limiting unresolved task Live/Stop controls to running cards
- retried current lifecycle reconciliation after a shared task refresh is invalidated by a concurrent card write
- cleared obsolete task-refresh errors when refreshed cards no longer need task data, and hid dismissed floating tooltips from assistive technology
- required linked-session abort success when a recorded task is already missing, preventing false blocked-state writeback
- honored task-refresh backoff for session-only cards while retaining session reconciliation, and preserved cached summaries on partial full-refresh confirmation failures
- re-armed polling across leave/re-enter teardown, cleared stopped-poll loading state, and discarded in-flight polls that overlap opening an edit draft
- removed an unreachable same-card details toggle while keeping the modal/inert interaction model intact
- hardened shared floating tooltip cleanup, viewport placement, accessible descriptions, and inherited native-title suppression
- made custom-select triggers announce both their field and selected value

## Verification
Proof was run on exact pushed head `9b617fbe4f7e2736c8cee9b9a90b4216c323ece5`.

- `git merge-tree --write-tree HEAD upstream/main` - clean against current `upstream/main` `a6aa84f2d0240a10d4b18150e09d363bb4cd186a`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- focused UI suite - 7 files, 305 tests passed
- Workboard browser E2E - 2 files, 2 tests passed
- scoped `oxfmt --check` and `oxlint` for the current-head lint repair
- current-head GitHub CI completed without failures; `check-lint`, `checks-node-core-ui`, and Real behavior proof passed

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Workboard operations layout and flows; serialized writes; guarded refresh, polling, polling teardown/re-entry, stopped-poll loading reset, edit-overlap poll discard, drag-and-drop, disconnect, and linked-task reconciliation; bounded paginated discovery; exact-confirmed missing-task handling; read-only task-refresh recovery; invalidated shared-refresh retry; session-only task-refresh backoff and reconciliation; cached-summary preservation on partial confirmation failures; obsolete and empty-board task-error cleanup; replacement-task polling/stopping; linked-session stop-success enforcement for missing tasks; unresolved-run start suppression; terminal unresolved-task Live/Stop suppression; custom-select keyboard/value accessibility; and shared tooltip lifecycle/viewport/accessibility behavior.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw PR branch at exact pushed head `9b617fbe4f7e2736c8cee9b9a90b4216c323ece5`, using the real local Workboard plugin state plus deterministic Control UI browser E2E. No live operator channel, protected channel, configured binding, or test fixture was mutated.

- `node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/app-lifecycle.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-settings.refresh-active-tab.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-tooltip-lifecycle.test.ts --configLoader runner`
- `node node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui-e2e.config.ts --configLoader runner ui/src/ui/e2e/workboard.e2e.test.ts ui/src/ui/e2e/workboard-status-persistence.e2e.test.ts`
- `node openclaw.mjs plugins list --enabled --json | <redacted summary>`
- `node openclaw.mjs workboard list --json | <redacted summary>`

Evidence after fix:

Exact-head tests and browser E2E cover guarded refresh, drag-safe atomic polls, polling teardown/re-entry, stopped-poll loading reset, edit-overlap poll discard, read-only task-refresh recovery, invalidated shared-refresh retry, session-only task-refresh backoff and reconciliation, cached-summary preservation on partial confirmation failures, terminal unresolved-task Live/Stop suppression, obsolete and empty-board task-error cleanup, paginated discovery cycling, failed-enrichment link preservation, unresolved-run starts, strict linked-session stop fallback, custom-select accessibility, and tooltip title/viewport/accessibility behavior.

target.title: #92664: [Bug]: read tool fails to read GBK-encoded text files on Chinese Windows (displays garbled text)
target.body:
## Summary

Fix garbled text when reading non-UTF-8 files (GBK on Chinese Windows,
Shift_JIS on Japanese Windows, etc.) by adding automatic encoding
fallback. No new API parameter — the read tool detects invalid UTF-8
buffers and falls back to common CJK legacy encodings transparently.

## Root Cause

The read tool's `execute` method decoded file buffers with
`buffer.toString("utf-8")` unconditionally. Files in legacy encodings
like GBK (Chinese Windows codepage 936) contain byte sequences that are
not valid UTF-8, producing mojibake (garbled text).

Rather than adding an explicit `encoding` API parameter (which creates a
new model-facing contract and requires maintainer API decisions), this
fix adds automatic detection: strict UTF-8 decode first, then fallback to
common CJK encodings via WHATWG `TextDecoder`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: The read tool now decodes text files automatically —
strict UTF-8 first, then fallback to legacy CJK encodings (gbk, gb18030,
shift_jis, big5, euc-kr). No API change, no new model-facing parameters.
All existing read calls work unchanged.

Real environment tested: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64, Node.js v22.21.1,
OpenClaw main @ b130251b5e, tsx for repro MJS, vitest for test suites.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sessions/tools/read.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.read.workspace-root-guard.test.ts
./node_modules/.bin/tsx scripts/repro-92664.mjs
```

Evidence after fix:

- `read.test.ts`: 3 tests passed
- `agent-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts`: 8 tests passed
- `agent-tools.read.workspace-root-guard.test.ts`: 16 tests passed
- `scripts/repro-92664.mjs`: 5 assertions, 0 failed
- GBK Chinese text correctly decoded without any API parameter
- UTF-8 files still work identically (backward compatible)

Observed result after fix:

```
=== #92664: Read tool automatic encoding fallback ===

1. GBK-encoded Chinese file (the REPORTED bug):
   Raw bytes: 0x47 0x42 0x4b 0x20 0xb1 0xe0 0xc2 0xeb 0xb2 0xe2 0xca 0xd4 ...
   Temp path: /tmp/openclaw-gbk-test.txt

2. read GBK file (NO encoding param — automatic fallback):
   Output:  "GBK 编码测试\n公司：深圳欧盛自动化"
   Verdict: ✅ CORRECT — automatic fallback restored Chinese text
  ✓ automatic fallback correctly decodes GBK Chinese file

target.title: fix(discord): forward tool-start events for status reactions when verbose is off
target.body:
## Summary

Discord intermediate status reaction emojis (🔧, 🔍, 🛠️, etc.) are never displayed during tool execution when verbose progress is off — the default configuration. Users see only 👀/⏳/⚠️/🧠 but no tool-phase indicators, making the bot appear frozen during multi-step tool chains.

### Root cause

`forwardFollowupProgressEvent` in `src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.ts` applied an early return when `emitChannelProgress` was `false` (which is the case whenever `shouldEmitToolResultProgress()` returns `false`, i.e. verbose level is not `"on"` or `"full"`). This early return skipped **all** non-compaction events — including `tool` stream events — so `opts.onToolStart` was never invoked for tool start phases.

Channel handlers (Discord, Slack, etc.) wire `onToolStart` to update status reaction emojis and draft progress. Because the callback never fired in default config, the status reaction controller's `setTool()` was never called, and tool-phase emojis were silently suppressed.

### Fix

Move the `onToolStart` invocation **before** the `emitChannelProgress` early return. Tool start events now always reach the callback, allowing channel handlers to update status reactions regardless of verbose level. The callback itself is safe to fire unconditionally because:

- Status reaction controllers check their own `enabled` flag
- Draft progress handlers check whether draft streaming is active
- The `emitChannelProgress` gate still correctly suppresses item events, plan updates, and other verbose-gated content after the tool event block

Updated 4 existing tests that asserted `onToolStart` was not called when verbose was off — these tests now assert the callback fires once (since status reactions should always update).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Discord (and other channels) status reaction emojis for tool execution phases (🔧, 🌐, 💻, etc.) were never displayed when verbose progress was off, the default configuration. Tool start events were filtered by `emitChannelProgress` before reaching `onToolStart`.
- **Environment tested:** macOS 26.4.1, Node.js, OpenClaw dev workdir at commit `eb1b6408` (upstream/main).
- **Steps run after the patch:**
```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/channels/status-reactions.test.ts
npx oxlint src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts
```
- **Evidence after fix:**
```
src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.ts:135-137: tool start events forwarded before emitChannelProgress gate
followup-runner.test.ts: 74 tests passed
agent-runner-execution.test.ts: 189 tests passed
dispatch-from-config.test.ts: 208 tests passed
message-handler.process.test.ts: 104 tests passed
status-reactions.test.ts: 43 tests passed
oxlint: 0 warnings and 0 errors
```
- **Observed result after fix:** Tool start events now reach `onToolStart` even when verbose is off. The 4 updated tests confirm `onToolStart` fires exactly once per tool start event. All 618 tests across the 5 affected test suites pass. Lint is clean.
- **What was not tested:** Live Discord bot interaction with real tool execution (requires Discord API credentials and a running gateway). The fix was verified at the unit test level by confirming `onToolStart` callback invocation behavior.

Fixes #92715

target.title: fix(tools): propagate external content provenance to policy hooks
target.body:
Summary:
- Adds structured external-content provenance to tool-call policy hook types.
- Propagates provenance through HookContext, before_tool_call plugin events, trusted tool policy events, and tool hook context.
- Detects EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT markers from the active embedded run prompt/session context.
- Refreshes provenance after same-run tool results through run-owned state keyed by `runId`, so fetch/browser/file external content is visible to later policy hooks even when wrapped tools use separate `HookContext` objects.
- Documents the public hook field and source semantics.
- Refreshes the plugin SDK type barrel so extension package-boundary cache keys invalidate when hook contract shapes change.

Why:
- External content is already wrapped as untrusted prompt text.
- Before this change, the post-model tool-call policy boundary did not receive structured provenance showing whether external content was present.
- Policy plugins could see the tool name, params, tool kind/input kind, run/toolCallId, derived paths, and session context, but not whether the tool call followed browser, web_fetch, email, webhook, file-fetch, or other untrusted external context.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed:
Same-run external content provenance reaches a later `before_tool_call` policy boundary after a `web_fetch` result introduces wrapped `EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT`, even when the two wrapped tools do not share the same wrapper-local `HookContext`.

This is the reviewed production failure mode:
- `web_fetch` runs first with one wrapper `HookContext`
- `bash` / exec runs later with a different wrapper `HookContext`
- both contexts share only `runId`
- the later exec hook must still receive `externalContent: { "present": true, "sources": ["web_fetch"] }`

Real environment tested:
Local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/tool-call-external-provenance`, head `0daf2ca1f70a8b9992f42f7f57ab5841ae465994`, running the real `wrapToolWithBeforeToolCallHook -> runBeforeToolCallHook` runtime path through the focused e2e regression.

```

Terminal capture / copied live output from the real local proof run:

target.title: fix(discord): disable ws 8.21.0 receiver part limits for gateway sockets
target.body:
## Summary

ws 8.21.0 introduced `maxBufferedChunks` and `maxFragments` receiver limits (commit `[security] Limit retained message parts`) that can trigger `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and close connections with code 1008 under certain traffic patterns.

This PR explicitly sets both limits to `0` (disabled) for Discord gateway WebSocket connections, restoring the ws 8.20.0 behavior for the trusted Discord gateway peer while preserving the security fix for all other WebSocket usage.

## Changes

- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.ts`**: Base class `createWebSocket()` now passes `maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0` with a `ws.ClientOptions` cast (since `@types/ws` 8.18.1 does not yet expose these fields).
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.ts`**: Extended `DiscordGatewayWebSocketCtor` type to include the new options; monitor gateway creation path passes the same disabled limits.
- **`extensions/discord/src/internal/gateway.test.ts`**: Added test verifying base class WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.test.ts`**: Added test verifying monitor plugin WebSocket receives the expected options.
- **`extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.proxy.test.ts`**: Updated two existing exact-match assertions to include the new options.

## Why `0` instead of raising the limits

- ws 8.21.0 treats `0` as disabled (`receiver.js:61`, `receiver.js:98`: `if (this._maxBufferedChunks > 0 && ...)`), matching the pre-8.21.0 behavior exactly.
- Discord gateway is a trusted peer; the security limits are designed to protect against untrusted senders.
- Choosing an arbitrary higher threshold would be fragile without real production traffic data to calibrate against.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: ws 8.21.0's retained-part receiver limits (`maxFragments` default 131072 / `maxBufferedChunks` default 1048576) abort a Discord gateway socket with `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` / close `1008`; this PR sets both to `0` (disabled) on the Discord gateway sockets, restoring ws 8.20.0 behavior for that trusted peer.
- **Real environment tested**: Live Discord gateway connection from a Docker `node:22-bookworm-slim` container (Node `v22.22.3`) with the repo-pinned `ws@8.21.0` installed exactly, using a real bot token (redacted), connecting to `wss://gateway.discord.gg/?v=10&encoding=json`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: build a `node:22-bookworm-slim` image with `npm install ws@8.21.0`, then run a client that constructs the gateway socket with this patch's options (`new ws.WebSocket(url, { handshakeTimeout: 30000, maxBufferedChunks: 0, maxFragments: 0 })`), completes the HELLO/IDENTIFY/READY handshake against the real Discord gateway, sends heartbeats, holds a 60s stable window, then closes with code 1000.
- **Evidence after fix**: Live Discord gateway run with this patch's options applied (token, `session_id`, and `resume_gateway_url` redacted):

```
[env] node=v22.22.3 ws=8.21.0
[discord] connecting wss://gateway.discord.gg (maxBufferedChunks:0, maxFragments:0)
[discord] open
[discord] HELLO heartbeat_interval=41250ms
[discord] IDENTIFY sent (token redacted)
[discord] READY received (session_id/resume_gateway_url redacted)
[discord] HEARTBEAT_ACK #1
[discord] stable window 60000ms complete
[discord] close code=1000 reason=<empty>
[result] PASS live: READY + 1 heartbeat ACK; no WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS during stable window
```

Supplemental deterministic mechanism repro against a local ws 8.21.0 gateway streaming one message in 131073 fragments (one over the client default `maxFragments`), exercising the same `ws.WebSocket` receiver path:

```
# default ws.WebSocket (ws 8.21 defaults)
[before] open
[before] error code=WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS message=Too many message fragments
[before] close code=1006 reason=<empty>

# this patch's options applied
[after] open
[after] message received: 131073 bytes - NO receiver-limit close
```

- **Observed result after fix**: On the real Discord gateway, with the disabled limits applied, the connection reaches `READY`, exchanges heartbeats, and stays connected through the 60s window with no `WS_ERR_TOO_MANY_BUFFERED_PARTS` and no `1008` close; the only close is the intentional client-initiated `1000`. The deterministic repro confirms the same constructor options remove the receiver-limit close that the default ws 8.21 client hits.
- **What was not tested**: the live run confirms a real gateway session stays healthy with the patched options, but does not force Discord's production fragmentation pattern on demand, does not exercise the `zlib-stream` transport, and does not drive the full OpenClaw plugin wiring end-to-end. The deterministic repro proves the ws 8.21 retained-part mechanism and that this patch's options disable the exact receiver checks, but uses a local peer rather than Discord's live framing.

## Tests

- `gateway.test.ts`: 25 passed
- `gateway-plugin.test.ts`: 17 passed  
- `provider.proxy.test.ts`: 21 passed
- TypeScript check (tsgo): clean

Fixes #88330

target.title: fix(control-ui): wire ui.seamColor from bootstrap config to CSS variables
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #56068. The `ui.seamColor` config option was accepted by the backend schema and exposed via `talk.config`, but the Control UI frontend ignored it because:

1. The HTTP bootstrap payload (`/__openclaw/control-ui-config.json`) never included `seamColor`
2. The frontend loader never read or applied it to CSS custom properties

This PR wires `seamColor` end-to-end: gateway bootstrap contract → HTTP response → frontend state → inline `:root` CSS variable overrides.

## Changes

- `src/gateway/control-ui-contract.ts`: add `seamColor?: string` to `ControlUiBootstrapConfig`
- `src/gateway/control-ui.ts`: forward `config?.ui?.seamColor` in the bootstrap payload
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/control-ui-bootstrap.ts`:
  - add `seamColor` to `ControlUiBootstrapState`
  - add `applySeamColor()` helper that sets `--accent`, `--accent-hover`, `--accent-muted`, `--accent-subtle`, `--accent-glow`, `--ring`, `--primary`, `--focus` via `document.documentElement.style`
  - removes those properties when `seamColor` is absent so `base.css` hardcoded defaults win
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/control-ui-bootstrap.test.ts`: add tests for CSS variable application, cleanup, hashless hex normalization, and complete removal coverage

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Issue #56068 — the configured `ui.seamColor` is now applied as the Control UI accent color; removing it falls back to the default red.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64, Node v22.19.0, Chrome 148.0.7778.215 headless, local OpenClaw source checkout.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Add `"ui": { "seamColor": "#00aaff" }` to `~/.openclaw-dev/openclaw.json`
  2. Run `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs --dev gateway`
  3. Open `http://localhost:19001/` in Chrome headless and dump the DOM root style
  4. Verify the bootstrap endpoint returns `seamColor` and the root element carries the derived CSS variables
  5. Remove `seamColor` from config, restart gateway, refresh page
  6. Verify the CSS variables are removed and the default red accent returns
- **Evidence after fix**:
  - Bootstrap endpoint returns `seamColor`:
    ```text
    $ curl -s http://localhost:19001/__openclaw/control-ui-config.json | python3 -m json.tool
    {
        "basePath": "",
        "assistantName": "C3-PO",
        "assistantAvatar": "🤖",
        "assistantAvatarSource": "avatars/c3po.png",
        "assistantAvatarStatus": "none",
        "assistantAvatarReason": "missing",
        "assistantAgentId": "dev",
        "serverVersion": "2026.6.2",
        "localMediaPreviewRoots": [
            "/tmp/openclaw",
            "/home/0668000666/.openclaw-dev/media",
            "/home/0668000666/.openclaw-dev/canvas",
            "/home/0668000666/.openclaw-dev/workspace",
            "/home/0668000666/.openclaw-dev/sandboxes",
            "/home/0668000666/.openclaw/workspace-dev"
        ],
        "embedSandbox": "scripts",
        "allowExternalEmbedUrls": false,
        "seamColor": "#00aaff"
    }
    ```
  - The configured blue accent is applied to the `:root` style (Chrome headless DOM dump):
    ```text
    $ google-chrome --headless --disable-gpu --no-sandbox --window-size=1280,720 \
        --virtual-time-budget=5000 --dump-dom http://localhost:19001/ \
        | grep -o 'style="[^"]*"' | head -n 1
    style="color-scheme: light; --radius-sm: 6px; --radius-md: 10px; --radius-lg: 14px; --radius-xl: 20px; --radius-full: 9999px; --radius: 10px; --control-ui-text-scale: 1.00; --accent: #00aaff; --accent-hover: color-mix(in srgb, #00aaff, white 15%); --accent-muted: #00aaff; --accent-subtle: rgba(0, 170, 255, 0.1); --accent-glow: rgba(0, 170, 255, 0.2); --ring: #00aaff; --primary: #00aaff; --focus: #00aaff;"
    ```
  - After removing `seamColor` and restarting the gateway, the derived variables are absent from `:root` and the default red accent returns (fallback screenshot):

    ![Control UI with default red seamColor](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/eae8f153-81f8-4ce1-b6ef-e2d29bae29cd)
- **Observed result after fix**: The bootstrap endpoint returns the configured `seamColor`, the `:root` element receives the derived blue CSS variables, and the Control UI renders blue accents. After removing `seamColor` and restarting the gateway, the variables are removed and the default red accent returns.
- **What was not tested**: Dark/light theme switching behavior; WebSocket `talk.config` path (already worked); live model chat (no API key in the test environment).

## Verification

- Unit tests: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/controllers/control-ui-bootstrap.test.ts` → 13 passed
- Build: `pnpm build` → passed
- Lint: `npx oxlint --import-plugin --config .oxlintrc.json src/gateway/control-ui-contract.ts src/gateway/control-ui.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/control-ui-bootstrap.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/control-ui-bootstrap.test.ts` → clean

target.title: feat(kimi): show code quota in usage status
target.body:
## Summary

Adds Kimi Code usage reporting to the existing provider usage pipeline.

- Adds a Kimi usage fetcher for `GET https://api.kimi.com/coding/v1/usages`.
- Parses Kimi Code `5h` and `7d` quota windows into the existing usage snapshot shape.
- Wires the bundled `kimi-coding` provider into `resolveUsageAuth` and `fetchUsageSnapshot`.
- Maps `kimi-code` and `kimi-coding` usage aliases to the new `kimi` usage provider.
- Surfaces Kimi API-key usage windows in `models.authStatus`, matching the existing DeepSeek API-key status enrichment path.

## Why

Kimi Code subscription users currently do not see quota usage in OpenClaw status surfaces. This is a focused follow-up to #67593.

A previous broader attempt in #20343 also tried to add Kimi/Moonshot usage, but it mixed unrelated quota UI and provider changes and was closed unmerged. This PR keeps scope limited to Kimi Code usage integration so `/status`, provider usage summaries, and plugin usage hooks can show the Kimi Code quota windows without changing unrelated providers or UI grouping behavior.

## Behavior

When a Kimi API key is available from the provider config/store or `KIMI_CODE_API_KEY`, `KIMI_API_KEY`, or `KIMICODE_API_KEY`, OpenClaw can fetch the Kimi Code usage payload and display:

- `5h` from the matching limit entry, using either label-based matching or duration metadata.
- `7d` from the top-level `usage` row.

The fetcher supports both `used/limit` and `remaining/limit` shapes and returns stable usage errors for auth failures or malformed responses.

The Kimi Code usage endpoint response shape is treated as a provider endpoint contract assumption: the live proof below verifies the current `/coding/v1/usages` behavior, while maintainers still own the decision to accept that external contract for bundled status reporting.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Kimi Code subscription quota usage should be fetched through the new Kimi provider usage integration and normalized into the existing OpenClaw provider usage snapshot, so status/usage surfaces can show the Kimi `5h` and `7d` windows instead of omitting Kimi.

**Real environment tested:** Local macOS OpenClaw checkout on PR branch `codex/kimi-code-usage-quota` at commit `1858f38c`, using the real local OpenClaw config at `/Users/alex/.openclaw/openclaw.json` with provider `kimi-coding` base URL `https://api.kimi.com/coding` and a real Kimi Code API key from config. Token redacted.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a local Node command with `node --import tsx` that imported this PR's `./src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.kimi.ts`, read the local OpenClaw Kimi provider config, and called `fetchKimiUsage(token, 10000, fetch, { baseUrl })` against the real Kimi Code usage endpoint.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the live command after this patch:

```json
{
  "proof": "real Kimi Code usage endpoint via PR fetchKimiUsage",
  "startedAt": "2026-06-13T09:25:23.283Z",
  "finishedAt": "2026-06-13T09:25:23.435Z",
  "baseUrl": "https://api.kimi.com/coding",
  "provider": "kimi",
  "displayName": "Kimi",
  "windows": [
    {
      "label": "5h",
      "usedPercent": 8
    },
    {
      "label": "7d",
      "usedPercent": 3
    }
  ],
  "error": null,
  "token": "<redacted>"
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The real Kimi Code endpoint returned a successful `kimi` usage snapshot with `displayName: "Kimi"`, parsed quota windows `5h: 8%` and `7d: 3%`, and `error: null`. This verifies the PR's live fetcher can authenticate with a real Kimi Code setup and normalize live endpoint output into the provider usage shape consumed by OpenClaw status/usage surfaces.

**What was not tested:** Full UI rendering was not exercised; this proof covers the live provider usage fetch path and parsed snapshot used by the status/usage pipeline.
## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 extensions/kimi-coding/index.ts extensions/kimi-coding/index.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.ts src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.kimi.ts src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.kimi.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.load.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.shared.ts src/infra/provider-usage.shared.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.types.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-usage.ts`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm plugin-sdk:check-exports`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm vitest run src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.kimi.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.shared.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.load.test.ts extensions/kimi-coding/index.test.ts`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions`

Follow-up for ClawSweeper P2 at `1858f38c`:

- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/models-auth-status.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.load.test.ts extensions/kimi-coding/index.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.kimi.test.ts`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm plugin-sdk:check-exports`

Note: full-repo `pnpm format:check` currently reports existing formatting drift on upstream main outside this PR's files, so this PR was validated with targeted `oxfmt --check` for the changed files.

target.title: fix(agents): do not misclassify client-disconnect abort as run timeout
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #90764 reports that embedded agent runs abort at ~165–180 s wall-clock regardless of `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` or `models.providers.*.timeoutSeconds`. The run exits with "Request timed out before a response was generated." and the message advises increasing `timeoutSeconds`, but increasing it has no effect on the abort timing.
- **Root Cause**: `onAbort` and `onExternalAbortSignal` in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` call `isTimeoutError(reason)` on the abort-signal reason to decide whether to set `timedOut = true`. `isTimeoutError` calls `hasTimeoutHint`, which calls `isTimeoutErrorMessage` — that function matches the pattern `/\boperation was aborted\b/i` against the error message. `AbortController.abort()` with no arguments produces `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")`; that message matches the pattern, so `hasTimeoutHint` returns `true` and `isTimeoutError` returns `true` for what is actually a plain client cancellation, setting `timedOut = true` erroneously. `watchClientDisconnect` in `src/gateway/http-common.ts` calls `abortController.abort()` with no args whenever the HTTP socket closes — for example, when a reverse-proxy or keepalive layer evicts the connection after a long period of no SSE output during a thinking phase. This fires `params.abortSignal`, which triggers `onAbort`, which then sets `timedOut = true` with no relation to the configured timeout values.
- **Fix**: Replace `isTimeoutError` with a new `isSignalTimeoutReason` export from `src/agents/failover-error.ts`. `isSignalTimeoutReason` checks only `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` — the name produced exclusively by `AbortSignal.timeout()` and `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`, the two intentional timeout-creation paths. Plain `AbortError` (`name = "AbortError"`) is a cancellation and returns `false`. `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all other callers that classify general LLM error messages, where the Ollama NDJSON / stream-abort patterns still apply.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/failover-error.ts` — export `isSignalTimeoutReason`: checks only `name === "TimeoutError"`, no text-matching heuristic.
  - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` — replace `isTimeoutError` import and both abort-signal handler call sites (`onExternalAbortSignal` and `onAbort`) with `isSignalTimeoutReason`.
  - `src/agents/failover-error.test.ts` — add `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe block: plain `AbortError` DOMException, ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE `AbortError`, `AbortSignal.timeout()` DOMException, `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`-style Error, null/undefined.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - `isTimeoutError` and its text-matching heuristics are unchanged; the change is scoped to the two abort-signal handler call sites.
  - Config surface unchanged (no schema, defaults, doctor migrations, or `docs/reference/config`).
  - Plugin surface unchanged (no plugin SDK, manifest, `extensions/*` api/runtime-api, registry/loader).
  - Gateway protocol unchanged.

### Reproduction

1. Configure `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds: 600` and `models.providers.custom-api-anthropic-com.timeoutSeconds: 600`.
2. Start a run that completes several tool calls, then enters a thinking phase that produces no SSE output for long enough to trigger the client's HTTP connection idle timeout.
3. The reverse proxy or keepalive layer evicts the idle connection; `watchClientDisconnect` fires `abortController.abort()` with no args.
4. **Before this PR**: `onAbort` calls `isTimeoutError(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` — `hasTimeoutHint` matches the message via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` (`/\boperation was aborted\b/i`), returns `true`; `timedOut = true`; run exits at ~165–180 s with "Request timed out before a response was generated."; increasing `timeoutSeconds` has no effect.
5. **After this PR**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` checks `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` → `false`; `timedOut` is not set; the abort is treated as an external cancellation, not a run timeout.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#90764)**: after the fix, a client-disconnect abort no longer sets `timedOut = true` in the embedded attempt; only abort-signal reasons with `name === "TimeoutError"` (from `AbortSignal.timeout()` or `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`) are treated as run timeouts.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node 22 — Vitest against the production `isSignalTimeoutReason` and the two production abort-signal handler paths)**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` driven directly with each discriminating input; `onAbort` / `onExternalAbortSignal` call sites exercised via the existing full-runner `attempt.test.ts` suite.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts`; `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.test.ts`; `pnpm format:check` and `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on the three changed files; `pnpm tsgo`.

**Evidence after fix**:

```
failover-error.test.ts   Tests  88 passed (88)
attempt.test.ts          Tests  121 passed (121)
```

The `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe covers: `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")` → `false` (watchClientDisconnect regression case; old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` matching `/\boperation was aborted\b/i`); `Error("request aborted", name="AbortError")` → `false` (old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE`); `DOMException("signal timed out", "TimeoutError")` → `true`; `Error("request timed out", name="TimeoutError")` → `true`; null/undefined → `false`.

**Observed result after fix**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(new DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` returns `false`; the abort-signal handlers classify client disconnections as `externalAbort=true / timedOut=false`; the run does not surface "Request timed out" for network-layer connection evictions.

**What was not tested**: live gateway repro with a real reverse-proxy idle eviction end-to-end; the abort-signal handler logic and the `isSignalTimeoutReason` discriminator are covered deterministically against the production functions, but a full network-layer round-trip was not driven.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` is strictly narrower than `isTimeoutError` for the two affected call sites; a timeout path that passes `params.abortSignal` with an error whose `name` is not `"TimeoutError"` will no longer be classified as a timeout.
- **Mitigation**: both intentional internal timeout-creation paths (`makeTimeoutAbortReason()` and `AbortSignal.timeout()`) produce `name === "TimeoutError"`; `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all LLM error classification callers where the broader text-matching heuristic is appropriate.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Agent runtime
- [x] Tests

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #90764

target.title: [AI-assisted] Persist agent-run error replies in chat history
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: A started Control UI agent run could surface a terminal provider/runtime error live, but `chat.history` after refresh did not retain an assistant error turn for that failed run.
- Solution: Treat agent-run failure replies as real error payloads and make Gateway persist exactly one display-safe assistant error transcript entry for returned agent errors, even when the runtime emitted only an internal error stub.
- What changed: Runner failure payloads are marked `isError`, Gateway appends a deduped `<runId>:assistant-error` transcript entry for returned agent errors, and regression coverage now locks the returned-error, runtime-stub, and duplicate/idempotency paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No provider behavior changes, no delivery routing changes, no config/lifecycle changes, no raw transcript writes, and no dependency or lockfile changes.

## Motivation

- A user should not lose the visible assistant error after refreshing the Control UI. If a started run fails before normal assistant output, the live error event and durable history should agree.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #89197
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A real Gateway/WebSocket Control UI run that fails at provider transport now leaves exactly one assistant error in refreshed `chat.history`.
- Real environment tested: Disposable Linux OpenClaw checkout with the patched PR branch, real Gateway server startup, real WebSocket RPC client, channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, and an intentionally unreachable OpenAI-compatible base URL to trigger the provider failure without secrets.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -- -t 'assistant error|returned agent-run error payloads|runtime emitted an internal error stub'
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.error-broadcast.test.ts src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts -- -t 'Agent failed before reply|assistant error|fallback candidate|error stub'
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node --import tsx ../real-chat-history-proof.ts after-gateway-force-v3
/usr/bin/time -v node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
Real runtime proof:
- provider transport failed with fetch failed / Connection error
- agent.wait status: error
- immediateHistoryCount: 3
- refreshHistoryCount: 3
- immediateAssistantCount: 1
- refreshAssistantCount: 1
- chatErrorEventCount: 1
- result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY

Focused Gateway proof: 3 passed, 102 skipped.
Adjacent Gateway proof: 107 passed.
Session tool result guard proof: 35 passed.
Runner/Followup focused proof: 8 passed, 214 skipped.
git diff --check: clean.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live provider failure still broadcasts the expected chat error, and both immediate and post-reconnect `chat.history` contain one assistant error text: `Agent failed before reply: LLM request failed: network connection error.`
- What was not tested: Full repository-wide test suite, successful `tsgo` core on this testserver, real provider credentials, and a browser screenshot.
- Before evidence:

```text
Unpatched main proof for the same real Gateway/WebSocket/provider-failure path:
- sendStatus: started
- agent.wait status: ok
- immediateHistoryCount: 2
- refreshHistoryCount: 2
- immediateAssistantCount: 0
- refreshAssistantCount: 0
- chatErrorEventCount: 0
- result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Runner final failure replies were not consistently marked as error payloads, and Gateway treated the runtime assistant-error callback as if it guaranteed a Control UI-visible `chat.history` assistant turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Previous coverage asserted live error broadcast behavior but did not prove refreshed `chat.history` retained an assistant error after a started real runtime failure.
- Contributing context (if known): Runtime/session guard error stubs can be internal transcript-only markers; Gateway still needs to durably mirror returned terminal agent errors into the Control UI history path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`, and the temporary real Gateway/WebSocket proof harness.
- Scenario the test should lock in: A started run returns an agent error; Gateway appends one assistant error transcript entry, keeps the live error terminal event, survives reconnect/history refresh, and does not duplicate the assistant error.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The source tests cover the exact Gateway and runner seams, while the real proof exercises server startup, WebSocket RPC, provider transport failure, `agent.wait`, and refreshed `chat.history` in one flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing broadcast tests covered visible error delivery but not durable assistant transcript history.
- If no new test is added, why not: New regression assertions were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Control UI users should now see the same failed assistant turn after refresh that they saw live when a started agent run fails before normal assistant output.

## Diagram

```text
real provider/runtime failure
  -> runner returns final failure payload marked isError
  -> Gateway returnedAgentErrorPayloads branch
  -> append one sanitized assistant error transcript entry with <runId>:assistant-error
  -> broadcast existing chat error terminal event
  -> reconnect chat.history still includes one assistant error
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No new scope; the change writes a sanitized assistant error to the same session transcript path already used for Gateway assistant persistence.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Not applicable.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux disposable test checkout
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw repository scripts with installed dependencies
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible provider configured to an unreachable local base URL for deterministic transport failure; no real credentials used
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI-style Gateway WebSocket RPC; external channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`
- Relevant config (redacted): Dummy redacted API key, local unreachable base URL, temporary state directory

### Steps

1. Start the real Gateway server from the patched checkout.
2. Connect a WebSocket client and send `chat.send` for a Control UI session.
3. Trigger a deterministic provider transport failure through the unreachable model base URL.
4. Wait for the run with `agent.wait`.
5. Read `chat.history` immediately.
6. Reconnect a fresh WebSocket client and read `chat.history` again.
7. Count assistant text messages and chat error events.

### Expected

- The run surfaces a live terminal error.
- Refreshed `chat.history` contains exactly one assistant error message for the failed run.
- The assistant error is not duplicated.

### Actual

- Before: refreshed `chat.history` had zero assistant messages for the failed run.
- After: refreshed `chat.history` had exactly one assistant error message and one chat error event.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
Before real proof result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
After real proof result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY
After source proof: Gateway focused 3/3, Gateway adjacent 107/107, session guard 35/35, Runner/Followup focused 8/8.
TypeScript core check on the testserver did not produce diagnostics; the process was terminated by signal 9 at about 1.78 GB RSS, so this is reported as a testserver resource blocker rather than a green typecheck claim.
```

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: Returned agent-run error payload detection, runner final failure `isError` marking, Gateway assistant-error transcript append, runtime internal error-stub callback interaction, idempotency key behavior, real provider transport failure, immediate history, refreshed history after reconnect, and existing chat error event behavior.
- Edge cases checked: Runtime internal error stub does not suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript; Gateway writes one `<runId>:assistant-error` entry for the returned terminal error; no dependency or lockfile files changed.
- What you did **not** verify: Full repo-wide suite, successful `tsgo` core on the resource-limited testserver, real external provider credentials, screenshot/recording, and production channels.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes.
- Config/env changes? No.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Not applicable.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A runtime-internal error stub could suppress the Control UI-visible returned error transcript.
  - Mitigation: Returned terminal agent errors now force the Gateway assistant-error transcript append while retaining the existing idempotency key.
- Risk: Gateway could duplicate assistant errors.
  - Mitigation: The transcript append uses a stable `<runId>:assistant-error` idempotency key and the source tests cover the one-message behavior.
- Risk: Error text could bypass display sanitization.
  - Mitigation: The Gateway fallback still uses `sanitizeAssistantDisplayText` before transcript append.
- Risk: Dependency Guard could require security approval.
  - Mitigation: No dependency manifests, lockfiles, or dependency blocks were changed.

## Summary
- separate local gateway reachability from follow-up detail/read probe failures
- add a bounded direct status-RPC fallback or degraded classification for local status/probe paths when the default probe times out or returns unknown capability but the gateway is otherwise RPC-capable
- add regression coverage for the #73535 split: default local probe false-negative while `--require-rpc` or direct `status` RPC succeeds

## Scope
This intentionally excludes unauthenticated loopback auth relaxation from #64400, which is routed to central security triage. The fix should stay in the ordinary status/probe classification and fallback path.

Fixes the non-security path tracked in #48360. Uses reproduction evidence from #62762 and #73535, with historical evidence from #51357 and #42019. Thanks @RacecarGuy, @justinschille, @DJBlackhawk, @tianyaqpzm, and @0xrsydn.

- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- Observed result after fix: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- What was not tested: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451
